Why is this so hard? Browser technology is amazing. It provides a powerful set of interface building blocks, ways to style and manipulate them, and tools to inspect and debug your

blocks, ways to style and manipulate them, and tools to inspect and debug your
applications. The software you write for the browser can be run on almost every
computer and phone on the planet.
At the same time, browser technology is ridiculous. You have to learn a
large number of silly tricks and obscure facts to master it, and the default
programming model it provides is so problematic that most programmers prefer
to cover it in several layers of abstraction rather than deal with it directly.

to cover it in several layers of abstraction rather than deal with it directly.
While the situation is definitely improving, it mostly does so in the form
of more elements being added to address shortcomings—creating even more
complexity. A feature used by a million websites can’t really be replaced. Even
if it could, it would be hard to decide what it should be replaced with.
Technology never exists in a vacuum—we’re constrained by our tools and

Technology never exists in a vacuum—we’re constrained by our tools and
the social, economic, and historical factors that produced them. This can be
annoying, but it is generally more productive to try to build a good under-
standing of how the existing technical reality works—and why it is the way it
is—than to rage against it or hold out for another reality.

Chapter 20
Node.js
So far, we’ve used the JavaScript language in a single environment: the browser.
This chapter and the next one will briefly introduce Node.js, a program that

This chapter and the next one will briefly introduce Node.js, a program that
allows you to apply your JavaScript skills outside of the browser. With it, you
can build anything from small command line tools to HTTP servers that power
dynamic websites.
These chapters aim to teach you the main concepts that Node.js uses and to
give you enough information to write useful programs for it. They do not try
to be a complete, or even a thorough, treatment of the platform.

to be a complete, or even a thorough, treatment of the platform.
If you want to follow along and run the code in this chapter, you’ll need
to install Node.js version 18 or higher. To do so, go to https://nodejs.org and
follow the installation instructions for your operating system. You can also find
further documentation for Node.js there.
Background
When building systems that communicate over the network, the way you man-

Background
When building systems that communicate over the network, the way you man-
age input and output—that is, the reading and writing of data to and from the
network and hard drive—can make a big difference in how quickly a system
responds to the user or to network requests.
In such programs, asynchronous programming is often helpful. It allows the
program to send and receive data from and to multiple devices at the same
time without complicated thread management and synchronization.

time without complicated thread management and synchronization.
Node was initially conceived for the purpose of making asynchronous pro-
gramming easy and convenient. JavaScript lends itself well to a system like
Node. It is one of the few programming languages that does not have a built-in
way to do input and output. Thus, JavaScript could be fit onto Node’s rather
eccentric approach to network and filesystem programming without ending up
with two inconsistent interfaces.

with two inconsistent interfaces.
In 2009, when Node was being designed,
people were already doing callback-based programming in the browser, so the
community around the language was used to an asynchronous programming
338
style.
The node command
When Node.js is installed on a system, it provides a program called node, which
is used to run JavaScript files. Say you have a file hello.js, containing this
code:
let message = "Hello world";
console.log(message);

code:
let message = "Hello world";
console.log(message);
You can then run node from the command line like this to execute the program:
$ node hello.js
Hello world
The console.log method in Node does something similar to what it does in
the browser. It prints out a piece of text. But in Node, the text will go to
the process’s standard output stream rather than to a browser’s JavaScript
console. When running node from the command line, that means you see the
logged values in your terminal.

logged values in your terminal.
If you run node without giving it a file, it provides you with a prompt at
which you can type JavaScript code and immediately see the result.
$ node
> 1 + 1
2
> [-1, -2, -3].map(Math.abs)
[1, 2, 3]
> process.exit(0)
$
The process binding, just like the console binding, is available globally in
Node. It provides various ways to inspect and manipulate the current program.
The exit method ends the process and can be given an exit status code, which

The exit method ends the process and can be given an exit status code, which
tells the program that started node (in this case, the command line shell)
whether the program completed successfully (code zero) or encountered an
error (any other code).
To find the command line arguments given to your script, you can read
process.argv, which is an array of strings. Note that it also includes the name
of the node command and your script name, so the actual arguments start at

of the node command and your script name, so the actual arguments start at
index 2. If showargv.js contains the statement console.log(process.argv),
you could run it like this:
339
$ node showargv.js one --and two
["node", "/tmp/showargv.js", "one", "--and", "two"]
All the standard JavaScript global bindings, such as Array, Math, and JSON,
are also present in Node’s environment. Browser-related functionality, such as
document or prompt, is not.
Modules

document or prompt, is not.
Modules
Beyond the bindings I mentioned, such as console and process, Node puts
few additional bindings in the global scope.
If you want to access built-in
functionality, you have to ask the module system for it.
Node started out using the CommonJS module system, based on the require
function, which we saw in Chapter 10. It will still use this system by default
when you load a .js file.
But today, Node also supports the more modern ES module system. When

when you load a .js file.
But today, Node also supports the more modern ES module system. When
a script’s filename ends in .mjs, it is considered to be such a module, and you
can use import and export in it (but not require). We will use ES modules in
this chapter.
When importing a module—whether with require or import—Node has to
resolve the given string to an actual file that it can load. Names that start with
/, ./, or ../ are resolved as files, relative to the current module’s path. Here,

/, ./, or ../ are resolved as files, relative to the current module’s path. Here,
. stands for the current directory, ../ for one directory up, and / for the root
of the filesystem. If you ask for "./graph.mjs" from the file /tmp/robot/robot
.mjs, Node will try to load the file /tmp/robot/graph.mjs.
When a string that does not look like a relative or absolute path is imported,
it is assumed to refer to either a built-in module or a module installed in a

it is assumed to refer to either a built-in module or a module installed in a
node_modules directory. For example, importing from "node:fs" will give you
Node’s built-in filesystem module. Importing "robot" might try to load the
library found in node_modules/robot/. It’s common to install such libraries
using NPM, which we’ll return to in a moment.

Installing with NPM
NPM, introduced in Chapter 10, is an online repository of JavaScript modules,
many of which are specifically written for Node. When you install Node on
your computer, you also get the npm command, which you can use to interact
with this repository.
NPM’s main use is downloading packages. We saw the ini package in Chap-
ter 10. We can use NPM to fetch and install that package on our computer.

can open it and look at the code. When we import "ini", this library is loaded,
and we can call its parse property to parse a configuration file.
By default, NPM installs packages under the current directory rather than
in a central place. If you are used to other package managers, this may seem
unusual, but it has advantages—it puts each application in full control of the
packages it installs and makes it easier to manage versions and clean up when
removing an application.

Package files

Package files
After running npm install to install some package, you will find not only a
node_modules directory but also a file called package.json in your current di-
rectory. It is recommended to have such a file for each project. You can create
it manually or run npm init. This file contains information about the project,
such as its name and version, and lists its dependencies.

When you run npm install without naming a package to install, NPM will

Versions
A package.json file lists both the program’s own version and versions for its
dependencies. Versions are a way to deal with the fact that packages evolve
separately, and code written to work with a package as it existed at one point

separately, and code written to work with a package as it existed at one point
may not work with a later, modified version of the package.
NPM demands that its packages follow a schema called semantic versioning,
which encodes some information about which versions are compatible (don’t
break the old interface) in the version number. A semantic version consists
of three numbers separated by periods, such as 2.3.0. Every time new func-
tionality is added, the middle number has to be incremented.

tionality is added, the middle number has to be incremented.
Every time
compatibility is broken, so that existing code that uses the package might not
work with the new version, the first number has to be incremented.
A caret character (^) in front of the version number for a dependency in
package.json indicates that any version compatible with the given number
342
may be installed. For example, "^2.3.0" would mean that any version greater
than or equal to 2.3.0 and less than 3.0.0 is allowed.

than or equal to 2.3.0 and less than 3.0.0 is allowed.
The npm command is also used to publish new packages or new versions of
packages. If you run npm publish in a directory that has a package.json file, it
will publish a package with the name and version listed in the JSON file to the
registry. Anyone can publish packages to NPM—though only under a package
name that isn’t in use yet, since it wouldn’t be good if random people could
update existing packages.

update existing packages.
This book won’t delve further into the details of NPM usage.
Refer to
https://npmjs.com for further documentation and a way to search for packages.

Streams
The response object that the HTTP server could write to is an example of a
writable stream object, which is a widely used concept in Node. Such objects
have a write method that can be passed a string or a Buffer object to write
something to the stream. Their end method closes the stream and optionally
takes a value to write to the stream before closing. Both of these methods can

takes a value to write to the stream before closing. Both of these methods can
also be given a callback as an additional argument, which they will call when
the writing or closing has finished.
It is possible to create a writable stream that points at a file with the
createWriteStream function from the node:fs module. You can then use the
write method on the resulting object to write the file one piece at a time rather
than in one shot, as with writeFile.

than in one shot, as with writeFile.
Readable streams are a little more involved. The request argument to the
HTTP server’s callback is a readable stream. Reading from a stream is done
using event handlers rather than methods.
Objects that emit events in Node have a method called on that is similar to
the addEventListener method in the browser. You give it an event name and
then a function, and it will register that function to be called whenever the
given event occurs.

then a function, and it will register that function to be called whenever the
given event occurs.
Readable streams have "data" and "end" events. The first is fired every time
data comes in, and the second is called whenever the stream is at its end. This
model is most suited for streaming data that can be immediately processed,
even when the whole document isn’t available yet. A file can be read as a
readable stream by using the createReadStream function from node:fs.

When we treat files as HTTP resources, the HTTP methods GET, PUT, and

interpret the path in the request as the path of the file that the request refers
to.
We probably don’t want to share our whole filesystem, so we’ll interpret these
paths as starting in the server’s working directory, which is the directory in
which it was started. If I ran the server from /tmp/public/ (or C:\tmp\public\

which it was started. If I ran the server from /tmp/public/ (or C:\tmp\public\

txt (or C:\tmp\public\file.txt).
We’ll build the program piece by piece, using an object called methods to
store the functions that handle the various HTTP methods. Method handlers
are async functions that get the request object as their argument and return a
promise that resolves to an object that describes the response.

When a request handler’s promise is rejected, the catch call translates the
error into a response object, if it isn’t one already, so that the server can send
back an error response to inform the client that it failed to handle the request.
The status field of the response description may be omitted, in which case
it defaults to 200 (OK). The content type, in the type property, can also be
left off, in which case the response is assumed to be plain text.

left off, in which case the response is assumed to be plain text.
When the value of body is a readable stream, it will have a pipe method that
we can use to forward all content from a readable stream to a writable stream.
If not, it is assumed to be either null (no body), a string, or a buffer, and it is
passed directly to the response’s end method.

You may be wondering why trying to delete a nonexistent file returns a
success status code rather than an error. When the file being deleted is not
there, you could say that the request’s objective is already fulfilled. The HTTP
standard encourages us to make requests idempotent, which means that making
the same request multiple times produces the same result as making it once.

Summary
Node is a nice, small system that lets us run JavaScript in a nonbrowser context.
It was originally designed for network tasks to play the role of a node in a
network, but it lends itself to all kinds of scripting tasks. If writing JavaScript
is something you enjoy, automating tasks with Node may work well for you.

is something you enjoy, automating tasks with Node may work well for you.
NPM provides packages for everything you can think of (and quite a few
things you’d probably never think of), and it allows you to fetch and install
those packages with the npm program. Node comes with a number of built-in
modules, including the node:fs module for working with the filesystem and the
node:http module for running HTTP servers.
All input and output in Node is done asynchronously, unless you explicitly

All input and output in Node is done asynchronously, unless you explicitly

use a synchronous variant of a function, such as readFileSync. Node origi-
nally used callbacks for asynchronous functionality, but the node:fs/promises
package provides a promise-based interface to the filesystem.

Chapter 21
Project: Skill-Sharing Website
A skill-sharing meeting is an event where people with a shared interest come

A skill-sharing meeting is an event where people with a shared interest come
together and give small, informal presentations about things they know. At a
gardening skill-sharing meeting, someone might explain how to cultivate celery.
Or in a programming skill-sharing group, you could drop by and tell people
about Node.js.
In this final project chapter, our goal is to set up a website for managing
talks given at a skill-sharing meeting. Imagine a small group of people meeting

talks given at a skill-sharing meeting. Imagine a small group of people meeting
up regularly in the oﬀice of one of the members to talk about unicycling. The
previous organizer of the meetings moved to another town, and nobody stepped
forward to take over this task. We want a system that will let the participants
propose and discuss talks among themselves without an active organizer.
The full code for the project can be downloaded from https://eloquentjavascript.net/
code/skillsharing.zip.

code/skillsharing.zip.
Design
There is a server part to this project, written for Node.js, and a client part,
written for the browser. The server stores the system’s data and provides it to
the client. It also serves the files that implement the client-side system.
The server keeps the list of talks proposed for the next meeting, and the
client shows this list. Each talk has a presenter name, a title, a summary, and
an array of comments associated with it. The client allows users to propose new

an array of comments associated with it. The client allows users to propose new
talks (adding them to the list), delete talks, and comment on existing talks.
Whenever the user makes such a change, the client makes an HTTP request to
tell the server about it.
355
The application will be set up to show a live view of the current proposed
talks and their comments. Whenever someone, somewhere, submits a new talk
or adds a comment, all people who have the page open in their browsers should

or adds a comment, all people who have the page open in their browsers should
immediately see the change. This poses a bit of a challenge—there is no way
for a web server to open a connection to a client, nor is there a good way to
know which clients are currently looking at a given website.
A common solution to this problem is called long polling, which happens to
be one of the motivations for Node’s design.
Long polling

be one of the motivations for Node’s design.
Long polling
To be able to immediately notify a client that something changed, we need a
connection to that client. Since web browsers do not traditionally accept con-
nections and clients are often behind routers that would block such connections
anyway, having the server initiate this connection is not practical.
We can arrange for the client to open the connection and keep it around so

We can arrange for the client to open the connection and keep it around so
that the server can use it to send information when it needs to do so. But
an HTTP request allows only a simple flow of information: the client sends a
request, the server comes back with a single response, and that’s it. A tech-
nology called WebSockets makes it possible to open connections for arbitrary
data exchange, but using such sockets properly is somewhat tricky.
356

data exchange, but using such sockets properly is somewhat tricky.
356
In this chapter, we use a simpler technique, long polling, where clients con-
tinuously ask the server for new information using regular HTTP requests, and
the server stalls its answer when it has nothing new to report.
As long as the client makes sure it constantly has a polling request open, it
will receive information from the server quickly after it becomes available. For

will receive information from the server quickly after it becomes available. For
example, if Fatma has our skill-sharing application open in her browser, that
browser will have made a request for updates and will be waiting for a response
to that request. When Iman submits a talk on Extreme Downhill Unicycling,
the server will notice that Fatma is waiting for updates and send a response
containing the new talk to her pending request. Fatma’s browser will receive

containing the new talk to her pending request. Fatma’s browser will receive
the data and update the screen to show the talk.
To prevent connections from timing out (being aborted because of a lack of
activity), long polling techniques usually set a maximum time for each request,
after which the server will respond anyway, even though it has nothing to
report. The client can then start a new request. Periodically restarting the

report. The client can then start a new request. Periodically restarting the
request also makes the technique more robust, allowing clients to recover from
temporary connection failures or server problems.
A busy server that is using long polling may have thousands of waiting
requests, and thus TCP connections, open.
Node, which makes it easy to
manage many connections without creating a separate thread of control for
each one, is a good fit for such a system.

point where they touch: the HTTP interface over which they communicate.
We will use JSON as the format of our request and response body. Like in
the file server from Chapter 20, we’ll try to make good use of HTTP methods
and headers. The interface is centered around the /talks path. Paths that

and headers. The interface is centered around the /talks path. Paths that
do not start with /talks will be used for serving static files—the HTML and
JavaScript code for the client-side system.
A GET request to /talks returns a JSON document like this:

"presenter": "Jamal",
"summary": "Modifying your cycle for extra style",
"comments": []}]
Creating a new talk is done by making a PUT request to a URL like /talks/

Since talk titles may contain spaces and other characters that may not appear

To support long polling, GET requests to /talks may include extra headers

To support long polling, GET requests to /talks may include extra headers
that inform the server to delay the response if no new information is available.
We’ll use a pair of headers normally intended to manage caching: ETag and
If-None-Match.
Servers may include an ETag (“entity tag”) header in a response. Its value is
a string that identifies the current version of the resource. Clients, when they
later request that resource again, may make a conditional request by including

later request that resource again, may make a conditional request by including
an If-None-Match header whose value holds that same string. If the resource
hasn’t changed, the server will respond with status code 304, which means “not
modified”, telling the client that its cached version is still current. When the
tag does not match, the server responds as normal.
We need something like this, where the client can tell the server which version

We need something like this, where the client can tell the server which version
of the list of talks it has, and the server responds only when that list has
358
changed.
But instead of immediately returning a 304 response, the server
should stall the response and return only when something new is available or
a given amount of time has elapsed. To distinguish long polling requests from
normal conditional requests, we give them another header, Prefer: wait=90,

normal conditional requests, we give them another header, Prefer: wait=90,
which tells the server that the client is willing to wait up to 90 seconds for the
response.
The server will keep a version number that it updates every time the talks
change and will use that as the ETag value. Clients can make requests like this
to be notified when the talks change:
GET /talks HTTP/1.1
If-None-Match: "4"
Prefer: wait=90
(time passes)
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
ETag: "5"

Prefer: wait=90
(time passes)
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
ETag: "5"
Content-Length: 295
[...]

comment, modify talks, and even delete them. (Since the internet is full of

Can you see how that works? Every iteration of the loop, node points to the
current sublist, and the body can read its value property to get the current
element. At the end of an iteration, node moves to the next sublist. When that
is null, we have reached the end of the list, and the loop is finished.

its head node. To get element N + 1, you take the Nth element of the list
that’s in this list’s rest property.

Robot efficiency
The main limitation of goalOrientedRobot is that it considers only one parcel

Robot efficiency
The main limitation of goalOrientedRobot is that it considers only one parcel
at a time. It will often walk back and forth across the village because the parcel
it happens to be looking at happens to be at the other side of the map, even if
there are others much closer.
One possible solution would be to compute routes for all packages and then
take the shortest one. Even better results can be obtained, if there are multiple

take the shortest one. Even better results can be obtained, if there are multiple
shortest routes, by preferring the ones that go to pick up a package instead of
delivering a package.

Is it a good idea to use NPM modules for things that we could have written
ourselves? In principle, yes—for nontrivial things like the pathfinding function

This shows that the way you structure your promises can have a real effect
on the way the work is scheduled. A simple loop with await in it will make

Promise.all makes it possible for multiple tasks to conceptually be worked on
at the same time, allowing them to make progress while files are still being
loaded. This can be faster, but it also makes the order in which things will
happen less predictable.

promise. That makes the promise returned by then fail, so the callback given
to the second then isn’t even called, and a rejected promise is returned from
the function.
Building Promise.all
The function passed to the Promise constructor will have to call then on each
of the promises in the given array. When one of them succeeds, two things

of the promises in the given array. When one of them succeeds, two things
need to happen. The resulting value needs to be stored in the correct position
of a result array, and we must check whether this was the last pending promise
and finish our own promise if it was.
The latter can be done with a counter that is initialized to the length of
the input array and from which we subtract 1 every time a promise succeeds.
When it reaches 0, we are done. Make sure you take into account the situation

When it reaches 0, we are done. Make sure you take into account the situation
where the input array is empty (and thus no promise will ever resolve).
Handling failure requires some thought but turns out to be extremely simple.
Just pass the reject function of the wrapping promise to each of the promises
in the array as a catch handler or as a second argument to then so that a failure
in one of them triggers the rejection of the whole wrapper promise.

To figure out which pixels to color, you can use the Pythagorean theorem.
First figure out the distance between the current pointer position and the start
position by taking the square root (Math.sqrt) of the sum of the square (x
** 2) of the difference in x-coordinates and the square of the difference in

** 2) of the difference in x-coordinates and the square of the difference in
y-coordinates. Then loop over a square of pixels around the start position,
whose sides are at least twice the radius, and color those that are within the
circle’s radius, again using the Pythagorean formula to figure out their distance
from the center.

Proper lines
The thing about the problem of drawing a pixelated line is that it is really four
similar but slightly different problems. Drawing a horizontal line from the left
to the right is easy—you loop over the x-coordinates and color a pixel at every
step. If the line has a slight slope (less than 45 degrees or ¼π radians), you
can interpolate the y-coordinate along the slope. You still need one pixel per

can interpolate the y-coordinate along the slope. You still need one pixel per
x position, with the y position of those pixels determined by the slope.
393
But as soon as your slope goes across 45 degrees, you need to switch the way
you treat the coordinates. You now need one pixel per y position, since the
line goes up more than it goes left. And then, when you cross 135 degrees, you
have to go back to looping over the x-coordinates, but from right to left.

have to go back to looping over the x-coordinates, but from right to left.
You don’t actually have to write four loops. Since drawing a line from A to
B is the same as drawing a line from B to A, you can swap the start and end
positions for lines going from right to left and treat them as going left to right.
So you need two different loops. The first thing your line drawing function
should do is check whether the difference between the x-coordinates is larger

should do is check whether the difference between the x-coordinates is larger
than the difference between the y-coordinates. If it is, this is a horizontalish
line, and if not, a verticalish one.
Make sure you compare the absolute values of the x and y difference, which
you can get with Math.abs.
Once you know along which axis you will be looping, you can check whether
the start point has a higher coordinate along that axis than the endpoint and

the start point has a higher coordinate along that axis than the endpoint and
swap them if necessary. A succinct way to swap the values of two bindings in
JavaScript uses destructuring assignment like this:
[start, end] = [end, start];
Then you can compute the slope of the line, which determines the amount the
coordinate on the other axis changes for each step you take along your main
axis. With that, you can run a loop along the main axis while also tracking

axis. With that, you can run a loop along the main axis while also tracking
the corresponding position on the other axis, and you can draw pixels on every
iteration. Make sure you round the nonmain axis coordinates, since they are
likely to be fractional and the draw method doesn’t respond well to fractional
coordinates.

Anurag Kashyap*1, Bhumika Verma*2, Tarun KumarSahu*3, TarkeshwarSahu*4,

ABSTRACT

Chhattisgarh, India 
ABSTRACT 
The craze for web development is rapidly growing as people like to be always online and do most things 
through the Internet. So, the demand for website or web app developers is rising. All the big and small 
businesses started using the internet to spread their products and services via websites. This paper lets us 
know when it started and what languages came into existence to assist web development. It describes how web

pages went from static to dynamic and with time it enhanced by acquiring designing skills and then responsive 
pages. This paper also provides information on many web technologies and associated frameworks. 
Keywords:Responsive, Static, Dynamic, Framework, Database, Artificial Intelligence, APIs (Application 
Programming Interfaces). 
I. 
INTRODUCTION 
Nowadays, web development has emerged as a vital component of our interconnected world. As businesses and

individuals increasingly rely on the Internet, the need for an effective web presence has become essential. Web 
development forms the backbone of creating engaging websites and interactive applications that cater to user 
expectations. Web Development allows users to communicate and share information and resources globally. 
People can show their innovations to the whole world and can get helped by interested ones who can support

them by providing capital or resources. With the help of Artificial Intelligence, it is now possible to track user 
behavior or interests so the developers can customize the content, recommendations, etc offered to the users. 
II. 
METHODOLOGY 
The first web page was created by Tim Berners-Lee in 1990, and it marked the beginning of the World Wide 
Web. The web page was created using a combination of three primary technologies: HTML (Hypertext Markup

Language), HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol), and a web browser.HTML is the markup language used to 
structure the content and define the layout of a web page. It allows the inclusion of various elements such as 
headings, paragraphs, links, images, and more. In the first web page, HTML was used to define the structure and 
text content.HTTP is the protocol that enables communication between web servers and web browsers. It

specifies how data should be requested and transmitted over the Internet. It was used to send the HTML 
document from the server to the web browser.For the first web page, Tim Berners-Lee used a web browser he 
developed called "WorldWideWeb" (later renamed Nexus). This browser allowed users to both view and edit 
web pages. 
A website typically consists of three main components: the front-end, the back-end, and the database. Each

component has its own role and responsibilities in the overall functioning of a website. 
Front-end 
The front-end refers to the part of a website that users interact with directly. It encompasses the user interface 
(UI) and user experience (UX) aspects of a website. Front-end technologies are responsible for presenting the 
website's content and design in a visually appealing and interactive manner.Here are the key frontend 
technologies: 
➢ HTML (Hypertext Markup Language) -

technologies: 
➢ HTML (Hypertext Markup Language) -   
HTML is the standard markup language used to structure the content of web pages. It defines the elements 
and their relationships, such as headings, paragraphs, images, links, forms, and more. HTML provides a 
semantic structure that allows browsers and search engines to understand and render the content 
appropriately.

appropriately.

HTML, initially created in 1991 by Tim Berners-Lee, was designed to facilitate the sharing of readable and 
accessible information through web browsers. However, it wasn't officially published until 1995 when the 
Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) released it as HTML 2.0. The early versions of HTML lacked

Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) released it as HTML 2.0. The early versions of HTML lacked 
advanced features like styling and rendering options, and they didn't support tables or fonts. 
HTML 2.0 introduced some additional features alongside those of HTML 1.0. In 1997, HTML 3.2 was 
developed, offering improved features for web page design. It included support for CSS (Cascading Style 
Sheets), allowing developers to have more control over the appearance and rendering of HTML elements.

HTML 3.2 also introduced support for frame tags, which were compatible with upgraded browsers at the 
time.  
HTML 4.01 followed HTML 3.2 and was released in 1999. It proved to be a successful version widely 
adopted by developers. HTML 4.01 expanded CSS support and introduced the concept of external styling 
sheets. This allowed developers to include CSS files using the <link> tag, enabling centralized and 
consistent styling across multiple web pages.

consistent styling across multiple web pages. 
The latest version of HTML is HTML 5, published in 2014. HTML 5 brought extensive support for new 
HTML tags and introduced significant enhancements. It is widely supported by modern browsers and has 
become the standard for web development. 
➢ CSS (Cascading Style Sheet) – 
CSS is used to control the presentation and layout of web pages. It defines the visual styles, such as colors,

fonts, sizes, spacing, borders, and positioning of HTML elements. CSS separates the presentation from the 
structure, allowing developers to create visually appealing designs and consistent styling across multiple 
web pages.Over the years, the W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) has controlled and developed CSS 
features through multiple specifications. CSS includes various profiles and levels, catering to specific

devices and user interfaces. These profiles are subsets of CSS levels and include dedicated profiles for 
mobile devices, printers, and television sets. It's essential to distinguish profiles from media types 
introduced in CSS 2. 
CSS 1 was initially released in 1996 and later republished in 1999 with corrections. CSS 2, introduced in 
1998, expanded CSS capabilities by supporting different output media.

1998, expanded CSS capabilities by supporting different output media. 
After CSS 2.1, CSS underwent modularization, allowing independent development of each module. This 
modular approach enables leveling of existing modules and the creation of new modules with new features 
or extensions to CSS 2.1. 
Currently, CSS consists of over 60 modules defining various capabilities. Some modules are already part of

the standard, while others are still in development. The term "CSS3" encompasses everything published 
after CSS 2.1, reflecting ongoing advancements. However, there isn't a single integrated CSS4 specification. 
➢ Javascript –  
JavaScript is a programming language that enables interactivity and dynamic behavior on web pages. It 
runs in the browser and allows developers to handle user interactions, manipulate the Document Object

Model (DOM), perform client-side validations, make asynchronous requests to servers (AJAX), create 
animations, and add interactive features to websites. JavaScript is widely used in frontend development to 
enhance user experience and create rich, dynamic web applications. JavaScript, initially named Mocha, was 
developed in 1995 by Brendan Eich, a Netscape developer. Subsequently, its name was changed to Live-

Script. Netscape made a strategic decision to rename Live-Script as JavaScript, capitalizing on the 
popularity of Java, just before the release of their web browser Netscape Navigator 2. This led to the 
introduction of JavaScript 1.0.In November 1996, Netscape collaborated with ECMA International to 
establish JavaScript as an industry standard. Netscape released JavaScript 1.1 in Netscape Navigator 3.

During this time, Microsoft entered the competition with its web browser, Internet Explorer 3 (IE 3), which 
included its own JavaScript implementation called JScript. Microsoft opted for the name JScript to avoid 
potential licensing conflicts with Netscape.Netscape commenced its partnership with the European 
Computer Manufacturers Association (ECMA) International in 1996 to standardize JavaScript. In 1997,

JavaScript 1.1 was submitted to ECMA as a proposal. Since then, the standardized version of JavaScript has 
been known as ECMAScript and is specified under ECMA-262.

Back-end 
Back-end or back-end web development refers to the server-side aspect of web development, which involves

handling the processing, storage, and retrieval of data for a web application. It is responsible for executing the 
business logic, managing user authentication, and serving content to the client side of the application.Back-end 
developers write code using popular programming languages such as Python, Ruby, Java, PHP, and Node.js. 
These languages provide the tools and frameworks necessary to build robust and scalable server-side

applications. Additionally, backend developers work with databases such as MySQL, MongoDB, and PostgreSQL 
to store and manage data efficiently.In addition to coding, backend developers focus on creating and managing 
APIs (Application Programming Interfaces). APIs serve as the bridge between the front-end and the back-end, 
allowing different components of the web application to communicate and exchange data. Backend

allowing different components of the web application to communicate and exchange data. Backend 
development languages rely heavily on the server. It receives client requests and responds with a response. The 
following programming languages and frameworks support server-side programming: 
➢ PHP (Hypertext Preprocessor) is a server-side scripting language designed for web development. It was 
created by Rasmus Lerdorf in 1994 as a collection of Perl scripts. In 1995, PHP 2 was released with

improved features and database interaction capabilities. PHP 3, released in 1998, introduced a complete 
rewrite with enhanced performance and expanded platform support. PHP 4, launched in 2000, brought 
significant improvements to object-oriented programming and the Zend Engine. PHP 5, released in 2004, 
introduced major language enhancements and improved error handling. PHP 7, launched in 2014, provided

significant performance improvements and introduced new features. The latest major release is PHP 8, 
launched in 2020, with notable additions like the JIT compilation and union types. PHP's simplicity, 
community support, and extensive ecosystem have made it a popular choice for web development. 
➢ Python is a widely-used programming language known for its versatility and simplicity. It was created by

Guido van Rossum in 1991. In web development, Python gained traction with the introduction of 
frameworks like Django in 2005 and Flask in 2010. Python's popularity in web development can be 
attributed to its readability, simplicity, and extensive library ecosystem. Python provides a wide range of 
libraries and packages for various web development tasks, such as working with databases, handling HTTP 
requests, interacting with APIs, and more.

requests, interacting with APIs, and more. 
➢ Django is a high-level web framework that follows the model-view-controller (MVC) architectural pattern. 
It provides a comprehensive set of tools and features for building web applications, including database 
integration, URL routing, authentication, and templating. 
➢ Flask, on the other hand, is a lightweight web framework known as a micro-framework. It offers essential

components for web development while allowing developers the flexibility to choose additional libraries as 
needed. 
➢ Node.js is a popular JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 JavaScript engine. It was created by Ryan Dahl 
and first released in 2009. Node.js revolutionized server-side web development by enabling JavaScript to 
run on the server, as opposed to just the browser.Dahl initially developed Node.js to address the limitations

of traditional web servers that relied on thread-based models. Node.js introduced a non-blocking, event-
driven architecture, allowing for highly scalable and efficient applications.In 2010, Node Package Manager 
(npm) was introduced, which became a vital component of the Node.js ecosystem. npm allowed developers 
to easily install and manage packages, making it easier to build complex applications. 
Frameworks-

Frameworks- 
Frameworks are pre-built software libraries or platforms that provide a foundation for developing web 
applications. They offer a structured and organized approach to web development by providing reusable 
components, predefined architecture, and a set of tools and utilities. It simplifies the web development process 
by abstracting common functionalities and providing ready-to-use solutions for handling tasks such as routing,

database integration, user authentication, form validation, and more. They promote code reusability, efficiency, 
and maintainability. 
1. Front-end Frameworks: These frameworks focus on the client-side development of web applications. They 
provide pre-built components, styling options, and JavaScript functionalities to create interactive and 
responsive user interfaces. Examples include React, Angular, and Vue.js.

2. Back-end Frameworks: Back-end frameworks primarily handle server-side development and provide tools 
for building APIs, handling requests, managing databases, and implementing business logic. Popular backend 
frameworks include Django (Python), Ruby on Rails (Ruby), Express.js (Node.js), and Laravel (PHP). 
Database 
Databases provide a structured and efficient way to store, manage, and retrieve data for web applications. They

serve as a central repository where information can be organized, accessed, and manipulated easily.Databases 
support various operations like inserting, updating, and deleting data, enabling users to modify the stored 
information as needed.Databases provide mechanisms for securing data, including user authentication, access 
control, and encryption, ensuring that only authorized users can access and modify the data. 
Implementation: 
•

Implementation: 
• 
Relational Databases: Relational databases organize data into tables with predefined schemas. They use 
structured query language (SQL) for defining, manipulating, and querying data. Popular relational database 
management systems (RDBMS) include MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and Microsoft SQL Server. 
• 
NoSQL Databases: NoSQL databases offer a non-tabular, flexible way to store and retrieve data. They are

suitable for handling unstructured or semi-structured data. NoSQL databases include document databases 
(e.g., MongoDB), key-value stores (e.g., Redis), columnar databases (e.g., Cassandra), and graph databases 
(e.g., Neo4j). 
Some databases are- 
1. MySQL: MySQL is an open-source relational database management system (RDBMS) that is widely used for 
web applications. It is known for its scalability, performance, and ease of use. MySQL is compatible with

various programming languages and is often used in conjunction with PHP. 
2. PostgreSQL: PostgreSQL is also an open-source relational database management system known for its 
robustness, reliability, and feature completeness. It supports advanced features such as complex queries, 
triggers, and stored procedures. PostgreSQL is commonly used in web applications that require advanced 
data management capabilities.

data management capabilities. 
3. MongoDB: MongoDB is a NoSQL database that stores data in a flexible, JSON-like document format. It is 
designed to handle large amounts of unstructured data and offers high scalability and performance. 
MongoDB is commonly used in web applications with rapidly changing data requirements or a need for 
horizontal scaling. 
4. Oracle: Oracle is a commercial relational database management system that provides enterprise-level

features, scalability, and reliability. It is widely used in large-scale web applications that require high 
performance and robust data management capabilities. 
Responsive Web design 
Website design plays a pivotal role in the development process by organizing content into graphical models 
that serve as blueprints for coding a website. With the ever-growing number of internet users and the

widespread use of mobile devices like smartphones and tablets, it is crucial to adapt website content to suit 
different screen sizes. However, creating separate versions of a website for each device would be impractical 
and expensive to maintain.  
In this scenario, the responsive design emerges as a solution, allowing software developers to create web pages 
that dynamically adjust to the dimensions of various devices. This approach ensures fast and optimized

rendering of web pages, delivering an optimal user experience across mobile devices, tablets, and desktops.  
Responsive design has become an essential aspect of website development due to the increasing number of 
internet users and diverse devices. This research study utilized a quantitative methodology, employing a 
questionnaire to explore the advantages and limitations associated with responsive web design. The results

underscore the importance of delivering a positive user experience and improving accessibility as the primary 
benefits of responsive design. However, challenges such as browser compatibility, longer loading times, and 
complexities in optimizing the user experience are recognized as limitations.

Static and Dynamic Web Pages 
Static Web Page-

[512] 
Static and Dynamic Web Pages 
Static Web Page- 
Initially, when web pages were created, then at that time, those were all static, which means the page remain 
fixed and unchanged unless the developer changes the code manually. The content displayed on a static web 
page is the same for all users and does not respond to any user input or interactions. These pages generally 
consist of HTML, CSS, Javascript, etc. 
Dynamic Web Page-

consist of HTML, CSS, Javascript, etc. 
Dynamic Web Page- 
Dynamic web pages are generated on the server side in response to user requests and interactions. It takes 
more time to display output than a static web page. The content on a dynamic web page can change 
dynamically based on various factors, such as user input, database queries, or real-time data. Dynamic web 
pages provide personalized user experiences. By utilizing user data, such as preferences, location, and browsing

history, dynamic pages can deliver customized content and recommendations. This personalization enhances 
user engagement and satisfaction. 
The introduction of dynamic web pages took place in the mid-to-late 1990s. During this time, scripting 
languages like PHP (1994) and ASP (1996) emerged, allowing developers to embed server-side code within 
HTML to generate dynamic content. These technologies provided a way to retrieve data from databases, handle

user input, and generate web pages. Since then, dynamic web development has continued to evolve, with the 
introduction of frameworks, libraries, and modern server-side technologies that provide more advanced 
capabilities, enhanced performance, and improved user experiences. It also powers e-commerce websites, 
enabling features such as product listings, shopping carts, inventory management, secure transactions, and

order processing. These dynamic functionalities provide a seamless online shopping experience for customers. 
III. 
MODELING AND ANALYSIS 
The model and Material are used is presented in this section. 
 
Figure1: Components of Web Development 
IV. 
Future Scope 
Web development has a great future ahead. Progressive Web Apps combine the best features of web and 
mobile applications, providing a seamless and app-like experience on the web. They offer offline functionality,

push notifications, and faster load times. The rise of virtual assistants and smart speakers has opened up new 
possibilities for web development. Voice user interfaces and conversational design will play a significant role in 
the future, enabling users to interact with websites and applications through natural language commands and 
voice inputs. AI and ML technologies have the potential to revolutionize web development. They can be used for

personalization, content recommendation, chatbots, virtual assistants, data analysis, and more. Web developers 
will need to incorporate AI and ML capabilities into their projects to create intelligent and adaptive web 
experiences. As the IoT ecosystem continues to expand, web development will need to accommodate the 
integration of web-based applications with various IoT devices. This includes developing interfaces, APIs, and

platforms that allow seamless communication and control between web applications and connected devices.

V. 
CONCLUSION 
This research paper aimed at providing knowledge of how web development evolves. It describes all the web 
technologies that came into existence like front-end technologies, backend technologies, frameworks, and 
databases. The findings of our study highlight the growing significance of responsive design so as to cover large

audiences using devices of various screen resolutions. We analyzed the history of technologies, to know their 
versions and features. The latest versions may introduce optimized algorithms, better memory management 
techniques, or enhanced rendering capabilities, resulting in faster and more efficient execution of code and 
rendering of web content.

INTRODUCTION
HTML (Hypertext Markup Language) is used to create document
on the World Wide Web. It is simply a collection of certain key
words called ‘Tags’ that are helpful in writing the document to be
displayed using a browser on Internet.
It is a platform independent language that can be used on any
platform such as Windows, Linux, Macintosh, and so on. To
display a document in web it is essential to mark-up the different

display a document in web it is essential to mark-up the different
elements (headings, paragraphs, tables, and so on) of the
document with the HTML tags. To view a mark-up document,
user has to open the document in a browser. A browser
understands and interpret the HTML tags, identifies the structure
of the document (which part are which) and makes decision about
presentation (how the parts look) of the document.
HTML also provides tags to make the document look attractive

HTML also provides tags to make the document look attractive
using graphics, font size and colors. User can make a link to the
other document or the different section of the same document
by creating Hypertext Links also known as Hyperlinks.

Understanding Image Maps
Image Maps as, the name suggests, means mapping/linking to
different URL’s different portion of one image. Clicking on a
portion of an image, one can jump to the link connected with that
part.
For example: Different areas in the map of India may be linked
to the URL of website of the respective states.
Position of an image that acts as a link to different URL can be of

Position of an image that acts as a link to different URL can be of
different shapes and sizes depending on one choice. Possible
shapes are circle, oval, rectangle, polygon and point. Image maps
are widely used in Web pages. They are mainly used with
geographic maps, organization charts, drawing of multi-
component structures and so on. Best view in the site: http://
planningcommission.nic.in. An image map can be created by

Q1: What does HTML stand for?
A1: HyperText Markup Language
Q2: Name three semantic HTML tags.
A2: <header>, <footer>, <article>
Q3: Difference between class and id in HTML?
A3: id is unique per page; class can be reused.
Q4: What is the CSS box model?
A4: Content, padding, border, margin
Q5: Difference between relative, absolute, fixed, and sticky positioning?
A5: Relative = relative to itself, Absolute = relative to nearest positioned ancestor, Fixed = relative to

viewport, Sticky = toggles between relative and fixed
Q6: What is the difference between let and var in JavaScript?
A6: var is function-scoped, let is block-scoped
Q7: Name two ways to select elements in JavaScript.
A7: document.getElementById(), document.querySelector()
Q8: How to make a webpage responsive?
A8: Use media queries, relative units, flexbox/grid
Q9: What is the DOM?
A9: Document Object Model; represents HTML as objects JS can manipulate

Q9: What is the DOM?
A9: Document Object Model; represents HTML as objects JS can manipulate
Q10: Name a method to fetch data in JavaScript.
A10: fetch() API
Q11: What is a pseudo-class in CSS?
A11: Special states of elements like :hover, :focus
Q12: Difference between inline, block, and inline-block elements?
A12: Inline flows in text, block takes full width, inline-block behaves like inline but allows width/height
Q13: Explain event bubbling in JS.

Q13: Explain event bubbling in JS.
A13: When an event on a child element propagates up to parent elements
Q14: What are media queries?
A14: CSS technique to apply styles based on screen size or device
Q15: Name popular JS frameworks.
A15: React, Vue, Angular
Q16: What is the difference between relative and absolute paths in HTML?
A16: Relative is based on current file location, absolute is full URL or root-based
Q17: Explain flexbox main concepts.

Q17: Explain flexbox main concepts.
A17: display: flex; justify-content; align-items; flex-direction
Q18: Difference between local and global variables in JS?
A18: Local exists inside function/block, global exists throughout script
Q19: Name common input types in HTML forms.
A19: text, email, password, checkbox, radio, submit
Q20: What is the difference between GET and POST methods?
A20: GET appends data in URL, POST sends data in body (secure for sensitive info)
Q21: What is a CSS Grid?

Q21: What is a CSS Grid?
A21: Two-dimensional layout system using rows and columns
Q22: What is the difference between null and undefined in JS?
A22: null = no value, undefined = variable declared but not assigned
Q23: Explain the difference between == and === in JS.
A23: == compares value with type coercion, === compares value and type
Q24: What are responsive units in CSS?
A24: %, em, rem, vh, vw
Q25: What is the difference between inline CSS, internal CSS, and external CSS?

Q25: What is the difference between inline CSS, internal CSS, and external CSS?
A25: Inline = style attribute, Internal = <style> in head, External = linked .css file
